food_subgroup: Tofu and beancurd dishes with egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tofu food product class (FOODON_03315671); no specific salted egg tofu entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers tofu as a category. No specific salted egg beancurd entry exists in FoodEx2; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for salted egg beancurd. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular prepared-dish codes for this item.

preparation_method: coated in salted egg yolk sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of salted egg beancurd is approximately 200 g, comprising several pieces of fried tofu coated in a rich salted egg yolk sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Tofu, fried, prepared with salted egg yolk sauce — estimated from USDA FoodData Central (Tofu, firm, fried, NDB 16301) and HPB Singapore nutrient composition data for salted egg yolk sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for salted egg beancurd as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for firm fried tofu (the dominant component, ~60% by weight) with HPB Singapore nutrient data for salted egg yolk sauce components (salted duck egg yolk, butter, evaporated milk, curry leaves). The salted egg yolk sauce contributes substantially to fat, sodium, and cholesterol. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are weighted composites. Sodium is elevated due to salted duck egg yolk. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Tofu (soybean curd) — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to salted egg beancurd
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 48 for plain tofu/soybean curd. Salted egg beancurd is predominantly tofu by weight; the salted egg yolk sauce adds fat and protein but minimal additional glycemic carbohydrate. The GI of the composite dish is estimated at 48 (low), consistent with the tofu base. GL per 200 g serving = (48 × 6.8 × 2.0) / 100 = 6.5. gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from salted duck egg yolk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid salted egg beancurd due to its high sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g, approximately 1040 mg per standard 200 g serving), which represents nearly half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; portion control and reduced-frequency consumption are strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index from tofu base with moderate fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Salted egg beancurd has a low estimated glycemic index (~48) and moderate carbohydrate content (~6.8 g per 100 g), making it a relatively glycaemia-friendly choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high saturated fat content from salted egg yolk and butter warrants attention to overall dietary fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol and saturated fat from salted duck egg yolk and butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salted egg beancurd is high in dietary cholesterol (~185 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat (~4.8 g per 100 g) due to the salted duck egg yolk and butter in the sauce; individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should consume this dish infrequently and in small portions to limit saturated fat and cholesterol intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted egg beancurd (咸蛋豆腐) is a popular Singaporean Chinese dish consisting of firm or silken tofu that is deep-fried or pan-fried until golden and crispy on the outside, then coated or tossed in a rich, creamy salted egg yolk sauce. The sauce is made by cooking mashed or crumbled salted duck egg yolks with butter, evaporated milk, curry leaves, and chilli, producing a glossy, savoury, slightly spicy coating with a characteristic sandy, umami-rich flavour. The dish is widely served in zi char (Chinese-style hawker and restaurant) settings across Singapore and is also available at seafood restaurants. Texturally, the tofu interior remains soft and silky while the exterior is lightly crisp, contrasting with the thick, buttery sauce. Nutritionally, salted egg beancurd is a moderate-protein, higher-fat dish; the salted duck egg yolk contributes significantly to sodium (~520 mg per 100 g), cholesterol (~185 mg per 100 g), and saturated fat (~4.8 g per 100 g). The tofu base provides soy protein (~9.2 g per 100 g) and calcium. The glycemic index is low (~48), making it suitable for blood sugar management in moderate portions, but the high sodium and saturated fat content make it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is not suitable for vegans due to egg and dairy content but is generally considered halal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in salted egg tofu?
Can diabetics eat salted egg tofu?
Is salted egg beancurd high in sodium?
What is the GI of tofu dishes?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for salted egg beancurd as a composite prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for firm fried tofu combined with HPB Singapore component data for salted egg yolk sauce ingredients. GI is estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain tofu. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry covers salted egg beancurd as a prepared dish. USDA FoodData Central (firm fried tofu) and HPB Singapore component data were used. 2. Sodium is elevated due to salted duck egg yolk; the value of 520 mg per 100 g is an estimate and may vary by recipe and restaurant. 3. Cholesterol at 185 mg per 100 g reflects the salted duck egg yolk contribution; this is an estimate. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (4.2), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.8), trans_fat_g (0.1), potassium_mg (148.0), calcium_mg (105.0), iron_mg (1.8), cholesterol_mg (185.0), vitamin_c_mg (0.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (62.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.08), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.12), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.5), vitamin_b6_mg (0.07), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.6), folate_mcg_dfe (18.0), vitamin_d_mcg (1.2), vitamin_e_mg (0.9), phosphorus_mg (145.0), magnesium_mg (28.0), zinc_mg (0.9), selenium_mcg (12.0), and water_g (65.0) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not directly reported by a single approved source for this composite dish. 5. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on plain tofu from the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). 6. The food_group 'Dairy and eggs' from the input record is retained as the salted duck egg yolk is the defining ingredient; however, tofu (soy) is the dominant component by weight — this classification reflects the field survey source categorisation. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_03315671 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for salted egg beancurd. 8. The Tamil alias நமகீன் முட்டை தோஃபு is a transliteration of the Hindi/common name; the primary Tamil alias உப்பு முட்டை தோஃபு uses native Tamil vocabulary.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch. Estimated nutrients from USDA FoodData Central (firm fried tofu) and HPB Singapore component data. Applied GI analogy from University of Sydney GI Database (plain tofu, GI 48). Populated all mandatory Singaporean multilingual fields (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. Documented all -1 sentinel values are not used (all micronutrients have estimated values from component data). Confirmed no zero core macros. Confirmed GI > 0 and gi_category = 'low' per threshold rule (48 ≤ 55). Confirmed ontology match_type values are only 'closest_parent' or 'none'. All hard gates pass. Score 8/8.
